tagline: Model the impact of US dollar devaluation on your finances
keywords: US dollar collapse, dollar devaluation calculator, what happens if dollar collapses, dollar crash scenario
calcFn: var deval=parseFloat(devaluation)/100; var multiplier=1/(1-deval); var savingsReal=savings*(1-deval); var savingsLoss=savings-savingsReal; var groceryImpact=200*multiplier; var gasImpact=3.50*multiplier; var mortgageReal=mortgage*(1-deval); var goldOz=savings/2350; var goldHedge=goldOz*2350*multiplier; return {savingsReal:dollar(savingsReal), savingsLoss:dollar(savingsLoss)+' lost', groceryImpact:dollar(groceryImpact)+'/week', gasImpact:dollar(gasImpact)+'/gallon', mortgageReal:dollar(mortgageReal)+' (debt shrinks!)', goldHedge:dollar(goldHedge)+' (if you held gold)'};

faq.question: What would happen if the US dollar collapsed?
faq.answer: A dollar collapse would mean rapid purchasing power loss. Savings would buy less, imported goods would skyrocket in price, but debts would effectively shrink in real terms. Essential goods like food, fuel, and medicine would see the biggest price spikes.

faq.question: How can I protect against dollar devaluation?
faq.answer: Common hedges include gold/silver, real estate, foreign currency holdings, commodities, Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS), and Bitcoin. Diversification across asset classes is the most prudent approach.

faq.question: Has the US dollar ever collapsed?
faq.answer: The USD has never fully collapsed, but has experienced significant devaluations: the 1971 Nixon Shock (gold standard end), the 1985 Plaza Accord (40% decline vs major currencies), and periodic weakness during financial crises.

formulas: Import Price Impact = Current Price / (1 - Devaluation %)

tagline: See how inflation has eroded your dollar's buying power
keywords: purchasing power calculator, inflation purchasing power, dollar value over time, what was a dollar worth
calcFn: var years=endYear-startYear; var cumInflation=Math.pow(1+annualInflation/100,years); var equivalentToday=amount*cumInflation; var lost=equivalentToday-amount; var retained=(amount/equivalentToday)*100; return {equivalentToday:dollar(equivalentToday), purchasingPowerLost:dollar(lost), cumulativeInflation:fmt((cumInflation-1)*100,1)+'%', realReturn:fmt(retained,1)+'% retained'};

faq.question: How much has the dollar lost in purchasing power?
faq.answer: Since 2000, the US dollar has lost roughly 45% of its purchasing power. Since 1913 (when the Federal Reserve was created), it has lost over 96% of its value.

formulas: Future Value = Present Value × (1 + Inflation Rate)^Years

tagline: Calculate your share of the US national debt
keywords: national debt per person, US debt per capita, national debt calculator, how much do I owe
calcFn: var debtDollars=totalDebt*1e12; var pop=population*1e6; var tax=taxpayers*1e6; var perCapita=debtDollars/pop; var perTaxpayer=debtDollars/tax; var perHousehold=perCapita*householdSize; var annualInterest=debtDollars*0.032; var interestPerCapita=annualInterest/pop; var gdp=28.78e12; var debtGDP=(debtDollars/gdp)*100; return {perCapita:dollar(perCapita), perTaxpayer:dollar(perTaxpayer), perHousehold:dollar(perHousehold), interestPerCapita:dollar(interestPerCapita)+'/year', debtGDP:fmt(debtGDP,1)+'%'};

faq.question: How much national debt does each American owe?
faq.answer: With $34.5 trillion in national debt and 335 million people, each person's share is about $103,000. Per taxpayer (about 150 million), it is roughly $230,000.

formulas: Debt per Capita = Total National Debt / Population

tagline: Model hyperinflation impact on savings and daily costs
keywords: hyperinflation calculator, what if hyperinflation, hyperinflation scenario, currency collapse
calcFn: var cumulative=Math.pow(1+monthlyRate/100,months); var finalPurchasing=savings/cumulative; var annualRate=(Math.pow(1+monthlyRate/100,12)-1)*100; var breadPrice=5*cumulative; var gasPrice=3.50*cumulative; var lostPct=((savings-finalPurchasing)/savings)*100; return {finalPurchasing:dollar(finalPurchasing), annualRate:fmt(annualRate,0)+'%', breadPrice:dollar(breadPrice), gasPrice:dollar(gasPrice), lostPct:fmt(lostPct,2)+'%'};

faq.question: What is hyperinflation?
faq.answer: Hyperinflation is when prices rise more than 50% per month. It has occurred in Zimbabwe (2008), Venezuela (2018), and Weimar Germany (1923). It destroys savings and makes daily commerce chaotic.

tagline: Calculate real inflation impact on specific spending categories
keywords: CPI calculator, real inflation rate, inflation by category, cost increase calculator
calcFn: var rates={all:3.2,food:5.1,shelter:6.2,energy:3.8,medical:3.5,education:4.8,vehicles:1.2}; var rate=rates[category]||3.2; var cumulative=Math.pow(1+rate/100,years); var futureMonthly=monthlySpend*cumulative; var increase=futureMonthly-monthlySpend; var totalExtra=0; for(var i=1;i<=years*12;i++){totalExtra+=(monthlySpend*Math.pow(1+rate/100,i/12))-monthlySpend;} return {futureMonthly:dollar(futureMonthly)+'/month', increase:'+'+dollar(increase)+'/month', totalExtra:dollar(totalExtra)+' extra over '+years+' years', cumulativePct:'+'+fmt((cumulative-1)*100,1)+'%'};

faq.question: What is CPI inflation?
faq.answer: The Consumer Price Index measures the average change in prices for a basket of goods and services. Different categories inflate at different rates — shelter and food often rise faster than the overall average.

formulas: Future Cost = Current Cost × (1 + Inflation Rate)^Years

tagline: Calculate if your raise actually beat inflation
keywords: real wage calculator, wage vs inflation, did my raise beat inflation, real income
calcFn: var nominalRaise=newSalary-oldSalary; var nominalPct=(nominalRaise/oldSalary)*100; var realSalary=newSalary/(1+inflationRate/100); var realRaise=realSalary-oldSalary; var realPct=(realRaise/oldSalary)*100; var verdict=realRaise>0?'You got a real raise of '+dollar(realRaise):'You took a real pay CUT of '+dollar(Math.abs(realRaise)); return {nominalRaise:dollar(nominalRaise), nominalPct:fmt(nominalPct,1)+'%', realRaise:dollar(realRaise), realPct:fmt(realPct,1)+'%', verdict:verdict};

faq.question: What is a real wage?
faq.answer: Your real wage is your nominal wage adjusted for inflation. A 5% raise with 6% inflation means you actually have less purchasing power — a real wage decline of about 1%.

formulas: Real Salary = Nominal Salary / (1 + Inflation Rate)
Real Raise = Real Salary - Previous Salary

tagline: Calculate and compare national debt-to-GDP ratios
keywords: debt to GDP ratio, national debt GDP, government debt ratio, fiscal sustainability
calcFn: var currentRatio=(debt/gdp)*100; var futureDebt=debt*Math.pow(1+debtGrowth/100,yearsForward); var futureGDP=gdp*Math.pow(1+gdpGrowth/100,yearsForward); var futureRatio=(futureDebt/futureGDP)*100; var riskLevel=futureRatio>200?'Critical — Default/Crisis Risk':futureRatio>150?'High — Debt Spiral Likely':futureRatio>100?'Elevated — Sustainability Concerns':'Manageable'; return {currentRatio:fmt(currentRatio,1)+'%', futureDebt:'$'+fmt(futureDebt,1)+'T', futureGDP:'$'+fmt(futureGDP,1)+'T', futureRatio:fmt(futureRatio,1)+'%', riskLevel:riskLevel};

faq.question: What is a dangerous debt-to-GDP ratio?
faq.answer: There is no universal threshold, but ratios above 100% raise concerns. Japan has 260%+ but still functions. Greece defaulted at around 170%. The US is currently around 120%.

tagline: Model the impact of BRICS and global de-dollarization on your finances
keywords: de-dollarization calculator, BRICS currency, dollar reserve status, petrodollar, dedollarization
calcFn: var dollarImpact=reserveDecline*0.6; var annualImpact=dollarImpact/yearsToDecline; var cashSavings=savings*(1-goldAllocation/100); var goldSavings=savings*(goldAllocation/100); var cashLoss=cashSavings*(dollarImpact/100); var goldGain=goldSavings*(dollarImpact/100)*1.5; var netImpact=goldGain-cashLoss; return {dollarImpact:fmt(dollarImpact,1)+'% weaker', savingsImpact:'-'+dollar(cashLoss)+' in purchasing power', goldHedgeValue:'+'+dollar(goldGain)+' in gold appreciation', netPosition:dollar(netImpact), annualDrag:fmt(annualImpact,1)+'% per year'};

faq.question: What is de-dollarization?
faq.answer: De-dollarization is the global trend of countries reducing reliance on the US dollar for trade and reserves. BRICS nations (Brazil, Russia, India, China, South Africa) are leading efforts to trade in local currencies.

faq.question: Will the dollar lose reserve currency status?
faq.answer: Most economists expect the dollar to remain dominant for decades but gradually lose share. The USD's share of global reserves has declined from 72% in 2000 to about 58% in 2024.

formulas: Dollar Weakness ≈ Reserve Share Decline × 0.6 (approximate relationship)

tagline: Estimate the economic impact of a BRICS common currency
keywords: BRICS currency, BRICS impact on dollar, new world currency, BRICS gold-backed currency
calcFn: var scenarios={minimal:{deval:5,inflation:1,rates:0.5},partial:{deval:15,inflation:3,rates:1.5},full:{deval:30,inflation:6,rates:3}}; var s=scenarios[scenario]; var savingsLoss=savings*(s.deval/100); var foreignGain=savings*(investInForeign/100)*(s.deval/100)*0.8; var diversifiedValue=savings-savingsLoss+foreignGain+savings*(investInForeign/100); return {dollarImpact:'-'+s.deval+'% against major currencies', inflationBoost:'+'+s.inflation+'% additional inflation', interestRateImpact:'+'+s.rates+'% higher rates likely', savingsImpact:'-'+dollar(savingsLoss)+' in purchasing power', diversifiedValue:dollar(diversifiedValue)+' (diversified portfolio)'};

faq.question: Will BRICS create a new currency?
faq.answer: BRICS nations have discussed trade settlement mechanisms and potentially a gold-backed unit of account. A full reserve currency is unlikely soon, but bilateral trade in local currencies is already increasing.

formulas: Impact varies by scenario — models approximate based on historical currency transitions

tagline: Estimate recession likelihood based on economic indicators
keywords: recession probability, recession calculator, yield curve recession, are we in a recession
calcFn: var score=0; var yieldScores={normal:0,flat:15,inverted:35}; var unempScores={falling:0,stable:10,rising:25}; var inflScores={low:5,moderate:10,high:20}; var fedScores={cutting:10,holding:5,hiking:20}; score=yieldScores[yieldCurve]+unempScores[unemployment]+inflScores[inflation]+fedScores[fedAction]; score=Math.min(score,95); var timeframe=score>60?'6-12 months':score>40?'12-18 months':'18+ months'; var severity=score>70?'Moderate to Severe':score>50?'Mild to Moderate':'Mild if any'; var advice=score>60?'Consider defensive positioning: bonds, cash reserves, reduced leverage':score>40?'Maintain diversification, build emergency fund':'Stay the course with balanced allocation'; return {probability:score+'%', timeframe:timeframe, severity:severity, advice:advice};

faq.question: Can we predict recessions?
faq.answer: No model perfectly predicts recessions, but the inverted yield curve has preceded every US recession since 1955 with a 12-18 month lead time. This tool combines multiple indicators for a rough probability estimate.

formulas: Probability Score = Sum of weighted indicator scores (yield curve, unemployment, inflation, fed policy)

tagline: Calculate salary adjustments needed for cost of living changes
keywords: COLA calculator, cost of living adjustment, salary adjustment inflation, real income calculator
calcFn: var colaSalary=salary*(1+inflationRate/100); var newSalary=salary*(1+raiseReceived/100); var gap=newSalary-colaSalary; var realChange=((raiseReceived-inflationRate)/(100+inflationRate))*100; var monthlyImpact=gap/12; return {colaSalary:dollar(colaSalary), yourNewSalary:dollar(newSalary), gap:dollar(gap)+(gap>=0?' ahead':' behind'), realChange:fmt(realChange,2)+'%', monthlyImpact:dollar(monthlyImpact)+'/month'};

faq.question: What is COLA?
faq.answer: Cost of Living Adjustment (COLA) is a salary increase designed to keep pace with inflation. Social Security COLA for 2024 was 3.2%. Many employers provide annual raises that may or may not match actual inflation.

formulas: COLA Salary = Current Salary × (1 + Inflation Rate)

tagline: Track M2 money supply growth and its relationship to inflation
keywords: money supply calculator, M2 money supply, money printing, federal reserve money supply
calcFn: var futureM2=currentM2*Math.pow(1+growthRate/100,years); var totalIncrease=futureM2-currentM2; var excessGrowth=growthRate-gdpGrowth; var impliedInflation=Math.max(0,excessGrowth*0.7); var dilution=(1-currentM2/futureM2)*100; return {futureM2:'$'+fmt(futureM2,1)+'T', totalIncrease:'+$'+fmt(totalIncrease,1)+'T', excessOverGDP:fmt(excessGrowth,1)+'% excess growth/year', impliedInflation:'~'+fmt(impliedInflation,1)+'%/year', dollarDilution:fmt(dilution,1)+'%'};

faq.question: What is M2 money supply?
faq.answer: M2 includes cash, checking accounts, savings accounts, and money market funds. When the Fed 'prints money', M2 increases. M2 grew from $15.4T to $21.7T during 2020-2021 COVID stimulus.

faq.question: Does money supply growth cause inflation?
faq.answer: Generally yes, when money supply growth exceeds GDP growth, the excess tends to show up as inflation, though with variable time lags of 12-24 months.

formulas: Future M2 = Current M2 × (1 + Growth Rate)^Years
